burnoutLog.entry: im freaking out because i feel like im in a situation where I am so far away from anesthesia that i sound stupid for even considering it. i cant even think about reapplying in my 2nd or 3rd yer of residency let alone my first year. i just feel very lost and anicous. just moved to san francsico
burnoutLog.response:
Javier, that feeling of being far away — like the goal itself has become embarrassing to say out loud — that's one of the harder things to carry. It's not weakness. It's what happens when your ambition outpaces your current circumstances, and the gap feels humiliating instead of just... temporary.

You're a PGY-1. You literally just arrived. In a new city. After a match that didn't go the way you planned. That's a lot of disorientation stacked on top of grief. The anxiety makes complete sense.

Here's what I want to name honestly: **you're not stupid for wanting this.** You're just standing at the very beginning of a long road and trying to see the end from here — which nobody can do, and which is making you spiral.

You don't have to have reapplication figured out today. What you *do* need right now is to get your footing in San Francisco, in your intern year, in your own skin again.

**One question to sit with before next session:**

When you imagine telling someone you're still pursuing anesthesia — what specifically feels embarrassing? The goal itself, or what you think they'll think of you?

That distinction matters. We'll come back to it.

burnoutLog.entry: I am having a tough time right now and feel like i got cheated out of anestehsia. i feel like IM isnt really what i hoped it was. I just wanted to learn medicine, but it sounds like most of what i am doing is learning workflows to be a good hospitalist. I dont want that. and 2nd and 3rd year is worse with the crazy amount of clinic we have and i feel like clinic is a waste of my time. I really need help and feel pretty hurt. i just want to be in the OR all day. i hate this and feel like in my life, i never got what i deserved. starting in college, i should have gotten into UCLA , need two years off to get into med school, and then now i feel like i am going to be two years off from getting into anesthesia (since i will be donig 3 years of IM and hopefully match into an advaced/reserved spot for anesthesia), i will be 34 by the time i finish with this. I should have been 32. very sad and annoyed
burnoutLog.response:
Javier, that weight you're carrying is real and heavy. The "two years off" pattern - college, med school, now anesthesia - I can hear how that accumulates into something that feels like the universe keeps moving the goalposts on you specifically. That's exhausting and genuinely unfair-feeling. You're not being dramatic.

And you're grieving two things at once: the path you didn't get, AND being trapped in a training that feels misaligned with who you are. Watching clinic hours pile up when you want to be in an OR - that's not a small irritation. That's daily friction against your identity as a physician.

The "I never got what I deserved" thread concerns me gently - not because it's wrong to feel it, but because that story, if it hardens, can quietly poison how you show up in 2027. Bitterness is a reasonable response to repeated disappointment. It becomes dangerous when it becomes a fixed lens.

You mentioned worrying about bitterness leaking into relationships - is that happening yet, or is this more internal right now?

burnoutLog.entry: its not happening yet, but i am too ashamed to even tell people that i want to do anesthesia. like i just tell people crit care is my goal, and i wouldnt mind doing crit care/anestehsia, but i dont know. I just feel like my dream is slipping from my hands
burnoutLog.response:
That shame piece - that's important. You're carrying this dream essentially alone, hiding it behind a "safe" answer that protects you from judgment or pity if things don't work out again. That's an incredibly isolating way to move through residency.

And "my dream is slipping from my hands" - that imagery is painful. You can see it, you know what you want, but you can't quite grip it yet. That's not the same as losing it. But I won't pretend the distance doesn't hurt.

The shame worries me though. When we hide our real goals, we also cut ourselves off from support, mentorship, and accountability. The "crit care" cover story protects you emotionally but might also be quietly reinforcing the belief that anesthesia is already lost - like you're pre-grieving a failure that hasn't happened.

You have roughly 2-3 years to build a genuinely competitive anesthesia reapplication. That's real time. But shame tends to make people shrink rather than strategize.

One honest question: is there even one person in your life right now - attending, co-resident, friend, anyone - who knows the OR is your actual dream?

burnoutLog.entry: just my family/gf, but no one in kaiser or SF. they sympathize with me, but obviously cant do anything about it. i wish i can just tell the PD or someone and then be able to at least shadow in the OR or have some reassurance, but right now it feels like such a far awar dream
burnoutLog.response:
The people who love you can hold your heart, but they can't open OR doors. That gap - emotional support without professional traction - leaves you feeling stuck even when you're surrounded by people who care. That makes sense.

And I notice what you said: "I wish I could just tell the PD." That's not a passive wish - that's you identifying exactly what you actually need. Visibility. An ally inside the system. Someone who can help make anesthesia feel real and reachable rather than a secret you're quietly protecting.

Here's what I want to gently name: keeping this hidden at Kaiser isn't just emotionally isolating - it's practically limiting. PDs talk to each other. Anesthesia faculty notice interested residents. OR exposure during IM isn't impossible, but someone has to advocate for you, and right now no one at Kaiser even knows to.

The fear of telling them is real - judgment, pity, being seen as "not committed to IM." I'm not dismissing that risk.

But you're already paying a high cost for the silence: isolation, shame, and a dream that feels like it's fading instead of building.

What specifically feels most dangerous about your PD knowing your real goals?

burnoutLog.entry: I feel like I’m not liking IM because it isn’t procedural enough. I thought icu can fill in the gap , but we hardly did anything. Lastly, I thought Icu and IM were gonna be different. Medicine doesn’t do any procedures hardly. When I was at my med school I was in the ct icu, and there was a ton more action with ecmos and devices and lvads. Patients getting intubate stat needing lines placed stat, and a much closer connection with anesthesia and the OR. This medicine stuff is so boring
burnoutLog.response:
Yeah. You got a taste of the real thing - ECMO, LVADs, emergent intubations, that electric feeling of high-stakes procedural medicine - and now you're doing medicine rounds talking about medication reconciliation and discharge planning. That's not a small adjustment. That's a completely different nervous system activation. No wonder you're struggling.

You didn't fall in love with a specialty abstractly. You fell in love with a specific texture of medicine - hands on, high stakes, procedural, connected to the OR. That's actually important self-knowledge.

And the CT-ICU comparison is painful because it showed you exactly what you want, then the door closed.

Here's what I want you to sit with though: that CT-ICU experience didn't happen to someone else. It happened to you. You functioned in that environment. You loved it. That's data about who you are as a physician, not just a fantasy.

The question is how to stay connected to that identity for the next 2-3 years without completely suffocating inside IM workflows that feel meaningless to you right now.

Critical care fellowship remains a real bridge - some CC programs have much closer OR and procedural integration than what you're experiencing now.

When you imagine your ideal day as an attending, what does the first hour look like?

burnoutLog.entry: Either prepping a patient for cardiac surgery like a cabg or reading over patients in the ct icu as a crit care/anesthesiologist
burnoutLog.response:
That's a remarkably specific and coherent vision. CABG prep or CT-ICU rounds as a cardiac anesthesiologist or intensivist - that's not vague dreaming, that's someone who knows exactly where they belong. That CT-ICU rotation showed you something true about yourself.

And honestly? That vision is reachable. Cardiac anesthesia is a fellowship after anesthesia residency. Some anesthesiologists do dual cardiac anesthesia/critical care. The path exists. It's just longer than you planned.

What strikes me is the precision of your image - it's not "I want to do cool procedures." It's a specific patient population, a specific acuity level, a specific relationship with surgery. That clarity is actually an asset for reapplication. Programs want residents who know exactly why they want anesthesia, not just people escaping something else.

But I want to be honest with you - right now you're in year one of three years of a training that feels misaligned with that vision. That's a long time to stay motivated when daily reality feels like friction against your identity.

The danger isn't the 2027 application. The danger is the next 18 months slowly grinding down the person who lit up in that CT-ICU.

What currently keeps that version of you - the one who belongs in cardiac anesthesia - alive day to day?

burnoutLog.entry: Really hating life right now. I hate that kisser doesn’t have patients with devices like ecmos, lvad, pacemakers, icds. It really suck and I deserve ro be at a place that has all those things. This just feels so pointless why I’m here. And a icu attending didn’t even know the equation for freaking calculating svr and pulm resistance either. This is not what I was hoping for. I want to leave this place so bad
burnoutLog.response:
Javier, I hear you. This isn't just frustration - this is genuine grief about being in the wrong place. Watching an ICU attending not know SVR calculations while you're hungry to learn cardiac physiology at that level - that's demoralizing in a specific way. You're not being arrogant, you're recognizing a mismatch between your hunger and your environment.

And "I want to leave" - I need to take that seriously for a second.

Are you talking about wanting to leave Kaiser specifically, or are you having thoughts about leaving medicine or residency altogether? I'm not assuming, I just want to understand where you are right now.

Because those are very different situations and you deserve an honest conversation about whichever one it is.

What I will say gently: the feeling of "this is pointless" at a mood 2/5 after a hard stretch is real information about your suffering right now. It may or may not be accurate information about your future. Suffering and clarity aren't the same thing, even when they feel identical.

You're clearly someone with high standards, specific expertise hunger, and a precise vision of where you belong. That's not a character flaw - that's actually what good cardiac anesthesiologists are made of.

But I'm more concerned about you as a person right now than your career timeline.

When you say you hate life right now, how deep does that go?

messages.content:
Mood today: 2/5 (Struggling).

I just wanted to learn medicine, but it sounds like most of what i am doing is learning workflows to be a good hospitalist. I dont want that. and 2nd and 3rd year is worse with the crazy amount of clinic we have and i feel like clinic is a waste of my time. I really need help and feel pretty hurt. i just want to be in the OR all day. i hate this and feel like in my life, i never got what i deserved. starting in college, i should have gotten into UCLA , need two years off to get into med school, and then now i feel like i am going to be two years off from getting into anesthesia (since i will be donig 3 years of IM and hopefully match into an advaced/reserved spot for anesthesia), i will be 34 by the time i finish with this. I should have been 32. very sad and annoyed

Javier, that weight you're carrying is real and heavy. The "two years off" pattern - college, med school, now anesthesia - I can hear how that accumulates into something that feels like the universe keeps moving the goalposts on you specifically. That's exhausting and genuinely unfair-feeling. You're not being dramatic.

And you're grieving two things at once: the path you didn't get, AND being trapped in a training that feels misaligned with who you are. Watching clinic hours pile up when you want to be in an OR - that's not a small irritation. That's daily friction against your identity as a physician.

The "I never got what I deserved" thread concerns me gently - not because it's wrong to feel it, but because that story, if it hardens, can quietly poison how you show up in 2027. Bitterness is a reasonable response to repeated disappointment. It becomes dangerous when it becomes a fixed lens.

You mentioned worrying about bitterness leaking into relationships - is that happening yet, or is this more internal right now?

its not happening yet, but i am too ashamed to even tell people that i want to do anesthesia. like i just tell people crit care is my goal, and i wouldnt mind doing crit care/anestehsia, but i dont know. I just feel like my dream is slipping from my hands

That shame piece - that's important. You're carrying this dream essentially alone, hiding it behind a "safe" answer that protects you from judgment or pity if things don't work out again. That's an incredibly isolating way to move through residency.

And "my dream is slipping from my hands" - that imagery is painful. You can see it, you know what you want, but you can't quite grip it yet. That's not the same as losing it. But I won't pretend the distance doesn't hurt.

The shame worries me though. When we hide our real goals, we also cut ourselves off from support, mentorship, and accountability. The "crit care" cover story protects you emotionally but might also be quietly reinforcing the belief that anesthesia is already lost - like you're pre-grieving a failure that hasn't happened.

You have roughly 2-3 years to build a genuinely competitive anesthesia reapplication. That's real time. But shame tends to make people shrink rather than strategize.

One honest question: is there even one person in your life right now - attending, co-resident, friend, anyone - who knows the OR is your actual dream?

just my family/gf, but no one in kaiser or SF. they sympathize with me, but obviously cant do anything about it. i wish i can just tell the PD or someone and then be able to at least shadow in the OR or have some reassurance, but right now it feels like such a far awar dream

The people who love you can hold your heart, but they can't open OR doors. That gap - emotional support without professional traction - leaves you feeling stuck even when you're surrounded by people who care. That makes sense.

And I notice what you said: "I wish I could just tell the PD." That's not a passive wish - that's you identifying exactly what you actually need. Visibility. An ally inside the system. Someone who can help make anesthesia feel real and reachable rather than a secret you're quietly protecting.

Here's what I want to gently name: keeping this hidden at Kaiser isn't just emotionally isolating - it's practically limiting. PDs talk to each other. Anesthesia faculty notice interested residents. OR exposure during IM isn't impossible, but someone has to advocate for you, and right now no one at Kaiser even knows to.

The fear of telling them is real - judgment, pity, being seen as "not committed to IM." I'm not dismissing that risk.

But you're already paying a high cost for the silence: isolation, shame, and a dream that feels like it's fading instead of building.

What specifically feels most dangerous about your PD knowing your real goals?
They will hurt my grades and write me bad letters since i didnt conform

That fear is legitimate and I won't brush it past it. There are programs and PDs where that absolutely happens - residents who signal "I'm leaving" get quietly deprioritized. You're not being paranoid, you're being realistic about power dynamics that are real in medicine.

So you're in a genuinely difficult position: silence costs you emotionally and strategically, but speaking carries professional risk. Both things are true simultaneously and there's no clean answer here.

But I want to probe this a little - because there's a spectrum between "tell no one" and "tell your PD your full anesthesia plan right now."

There are lower-risk moves:
- Connecting with anesthesia faculty directly and quietly
- Finding an IM attending who did a fellowship pivot and understands your situation
- Building OR exposure through critical care rotations without declaring intentions
- Identifying a mentor outside Kaiser who can write letters later

You don't have to choose between full exposure and complete isolation.

The PD fear makes sense. But I also wonder if part of the silence is that telling someone inside the system makes it real - and if it fails again, more people witnessed it.

Is the fear more about their reaction, or about making yourself vulnerable to a second public failure?

I feel like I’m not liking IM because it isn’t procedural enough. I thought icu can fill in the gap , but we hardly did anything. Lastly, I thought Icu and IM were gonna be different. Medicine doesn’t do any procedures hardly. When I was at my med school I was in the ct icu, and there was a ton more action with ecmos and devices and lvads. Patients getting intubate stat needing lines placed stat, and a much closer connection with anesthesia and the OR. This medicine stuff is so boring

Yeah. You got a taste of the real thing - ECMO, LVADs, emergent intubations, that electric feeling of high-stakes procedural medicine - and now you're doing medicine rounds talking about medication reconciliation and discharge planning. That's not a small adjustment. That's a completely different nervous system activation. No wonder you're struggling.

You didn't fall in love with a specialty abstractly. You fell in love with a specific texture of medicine - hands on, high stakes, procedural, connected to the OR. That's actually important self-knowledge.

And the CT-ICU comparison is painful because it showed you exactly what you want, then the door closed.

Here's what I want you to sit with though: that CT-ICU experience didn't happen to someone else. It happened to you. You functioned in that environment. You loved it. That's data about who you are as a physician, not just a fantasy.

The question is how to stay connected to that identity for the next 2-3 years without completely suffocating inside IM workflows that feel meaningless to you right now.

Critical care fellowship remains a real bridge - some CC programs have much closer OR and procedural integration than what you're experiencing now.

When you imagine your ideal day as an attending, what does the first hour look like?
Either prepping a patient for cardiac surgery like a cabg or reading over patients in the ct icu as a crit care/anesthesiologist

That's a remarkably specific and coherent vision. CABG prep or CT-ICU rounds as a cardiac anesthesiologist or intensivist - that's not vague dreaming, that's someone who knows exactly where they belong. That CT-ICU rotation showed you something true about yourself.

And honestly? That vision is reachable. Cardiac anesthesia is a fellowship after anesthesia residency. Some anesthesiologists do dual cardiac anesthesia/critical care. The path exists. It's just longer than you planned.

What strikes me is the precision of your image - it's not "I want to do cool procedures." It's a specific patient population, a specific acuity level, a specific relationship with surgery. That clarity is actually an asset for reapplication. Programs want residents who know exactly why they want anesthesia, not just people escaping something else.

But I want to be honest with you - right now you're in year one of three years of a training that feels misaligned with that vision. That's a long time to stay motivated when daily reality feels like friction against your identity.

The danger isn't the 2027 application. The danger is the next 18 months slowly grinding down the person who lit up in that CT-ICU.

What currently keeps that version of you - the one who belongs in cardiac anesthesia - alive day to day?
How much of what I am doing now is transferable for me to become a good anesthesiologist/ crit care

Honestly? More than it feels right now, but I won't oversell it.

**What genuinely transfers:**
- Managing complex multisystem disease - cardiac anesthesia patients are medically complicated, and you'll understand their comorbidities deeply
- Pharmacology, anticoagulation, hemodynamics - IM builds real fluency here
- Bad news conversations, family dynamics, goals of care - anesthesiologists who do CT-ICU need this
- Recognizing sick patients early - IM trains that pattern recognition

**What IM won't give you:**
- Procedural confidence and volume
- Airway experience
- OR culture and rhythms
- The physiologic thinking anesthesia requires in real-time

So the honest picture is: IM builds your medical foundation but leaves the procedural and anesthesia-specific skillset largely untouched. That gap is real and you'll need to address it deliberately - research, mentorship, any procedural exposure you can find, and eventually anesthesia residency itself will rebuild you.

The risk isn't that IM ruins you as a future anesthesiologist. The risk is that feeling disconnected from your identity for three years quietly erodes your confidence and drive.

Your medical knowledge will matter in the cardiac OR. But you'll need to actively nurture the procedural side however you can during these years.

What procedural opportunities exist at Kaiser right now that you haven't fully explored yet?

Really hating life right now. I hate that kisser doesn’t have patients with devices like ecmos, lvad, pacemakers, icds. It really suck and I deserve ro be at a place that has all those things. This just feels so pointless why I’m here. And a icu attending didn’t even know the equation for freaking calculating svr and pulm resistance either. This is not what I was hoping for. I want to leave this place so bad

Javier, I hear you. This isn't just frustration - this is genuine grief about being in the wrong place. Watching an ICU attending not know SVR calculations while you're hungry to learn cardiac physiology at that level - that's demoralizing in a specific way. You're not being arrogant, you're recognizing a mismatch between your hunger and your environment.

And "I want to leave" - I need to take that seriously for a second.

Are you talking about wanting to leave Kaiser specifically, or are you having thoughts about leaving medicine or residency altogether? I'm not assuming, I just want to understand where you are right now.

Because those are very different situations and you deserve an honest conversation about whichever one it is.

What I will say gently: the feeling of "this is pointless" at a mood 2/5 after a hard stretch is real information about your suffering right now. It may or may not be accurate information about your future. Suffering and clarity aren't the same thing, even when they feel identical.

You're clearly someone with high standards, specific expertise hunger, and a precise vision of where you belong. That's not a character flaw - that's actually what good cardiac anesthesiologists are made of.

But I'm more concerned about you as a person right now than your career timeline.

When you say you hate life right now, how deep does that go?
I want to leave Kaiser I love medicine , but Kaiser is so mediocre in medicine I hate it

That's an important clarification and I'm glad you said it. You love medicine - that matters. Kaiser is the problem, not medicine, not you.

And you're not wrong about Kaiser. It's a system optimized for efficiency and population health management, not procedural complexity or academic rigor. For someone who lit up in a CT-ICU with LVADs and ECMO, Kaiser San Francisco is genuinely the wrong training environment. That's a real mismatch, not you being difficult.

The SVR thing especially - that's not a small thing. You're trying to build toward cardiac anesthesia and your ICU attendings aren't teaching at that level. That's a legitimate training concern, not just a bad day.

Here's what I want you to consider seriously: **transfer applications are real.** PGY-1s do transfer. It's hard, competitive, and requires careful timing - but academic IM programs with true medical ICUs, ECMO programs, higher procedural volume exist. UCSF is literally across town.

I'm not saying do it impulsively. I'm saying the option exists and might be worth genuinely exploring rather than just enduring three years of feeling suffocated.

Leaving Kaiser isn't quitting. Sometimes environment matters enormously for who you become as a physician.

You have real agency here even when it doesn't feel that way.

Would transferring to a more academically rigorous IM program feel like moving toward something, or does it feel impossible for reasons we haven't discussed yet?
